class="css-8atqhb"><ul class="css-1cgskve epjyd6m3"><li class="css-ccw2r3 epjyd6m2"><time class="css-1z1nqv e16638kd0" datetime="2010-09-27T12:48:13-04:00">Sept. 27, 2010</time></li></ul></div></div></div></header><section name="articleBody" class="meteredContent css-1r7ky0e"><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">THE FACTS</strong> Nothing but time can cure the common cold, but a simple cup of salt water might ease the misery this winter.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">A sore, itchy throat and respiratory congestion are some of the more common symptoms of a cold, and gargling with salt water seems to help for several reasons. A saline solution can draw excess fluid from inflamed tissues in the throat, making them hurt less, said Dr. Philip T. Hagen, editor in chief of the “Mayo Clinic Book of Home Remedies,” which is due out in October. Dr. Hagen pointed out that gargling also loosens thick mucus, which can remove irritants like allergens, bacteria and fungi from the throat.</p><div data-testid="imageblock-wrapper"><figure class="img-sz-small css-dtmmiv e1g7ppur0" aria-label="media" role="group"><div class="css-zgakxe erfvjey0" data-testid="photoviewer-children-figure"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Image</span><div class="css-nwd8t8" data-testid="lazy-image"><div data-testid="lazyimage-container" style="height:328.3692307692308px"></div></div></div><figcaption data-testid="photoviewer-children-caption" class="css-13ytnnu ewdxa0s0"><span class="css-1u46b97 e1z0qqy90"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Credit...</span><span><span aria-hidden="false">Christoph Niemann</span></span></span></figcaption></figure></div><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">In a randomized study published in The American Journal of Preventive Medicine in 2005, researchers recruited almost 400 healthy volunteers and followed them for 60 days during cold and flu season. Some of the subjects were told to gargle three times a day. At the end of the study period, the group that regularly gargled had a nearly <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240719180733/http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16242593" title="Study abstract." rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">40 percent decrease in upper respiratory tract infections compared with the control group</a>, and when they did get sick, “gargling tended to attenuate bronchial symptoms,” the researchers wrote.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Other studies <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240719180733/http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20088240" title="Sstudy abstract" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">have also found gargling helpful against sore throats and congestion.</a></p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">According to the Mayo Clinic, for best results, dissolve half a teaspoon of salt in a full glass of warm water and gargle the solution for a few seconds before spitting it out. Adults who want a more palatable remedy against cough and sore throat can try mixing warm water with lemon and honey. No need to spit it out.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">THE BOTTOM LINE</strong> Gargling with a saline solution can ease symptoms of a cold.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">ANAHAD O’CONNOR </strong>scitimes@nytimes.com</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div></section><div class="bottom-of-article"><div class="css-lojhqv">A version of this article appears in print on <span class="css-1dmwf73"> </span>, Section <!-- -->D<!-- -->, Page <!-- -->5<!-- --> of the New York edition<!-- --> with the headline: <!-- -->THE CLAIM: Gargling with salt water can ease cold symptoms.<span>. <a href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240719180733/https://www.parsintl.com/publication/the-new-york-times/">Order Reprints</a> | <a sore, itchy throat and respiratory congestion are some of the more common symptoms of a cold, and gargling with salt water seems to help for several reasons. A saline solution can draw excess fluid from inflamed tissues in the throat, making them hurt less, said Dr. Philip T. Hagen, editor in chief of the “Mayo Clinic Book of Home Remedies,” which is due out in October. Dr. Hagen pointed out that gargling also loosens thick mucus, which can remove irritants like allergens, bacteria and fungi from the throat."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":true,"index":1},{"__typename":"ImageBlock","media":{"__typename":"Image","altText":"","caption":{"__typename":"TextOnlyDocumentBlock","content":[],"text":""},"credit":"Christoph Niemann","crops":[{"__typename":"ImageCrop","renditions":[{"__typename":"ImageRendition","height":552,"name":"popup","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240719180733\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2009\u002F10\u002F12\u002Fhealth\u002F13really-650.jpg","width":650}]},{"__typename":"ImageCrop","renditions":[{"__typename":"ImageRendition","height":161,"name":"articleInline","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240719180733\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2009\u002F10\u002F12\u002Fhealth\u002F13really-190.jpg","width":190}]}],"id":"SW1hZ2U6bnl0Oi8vaW1hZ2UvMTg4YjgwOWMtMmU0My01OGExLWJiYzYtZDM4ZTVlODJkNDQy","imageType":"photo","legacyHtmlCaption":"","uri":"nyt:\u002F\u002Fimage\u002F188b809c-2e43-58a1-bbc6-d38e5e82d442","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240719180733\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.nytimes.com\u002Fimagepages\u002F2009\u002F10\u002F12\u002Fhealth\u002F13really.ready.html"},"size":"MEDIUM"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":true,"index":2},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"In a randomized study published in The American Journal of Preventive Medicine in 2005, researchers recruited almost 400 healthy volunteers and followed them for 60 days during cold and flu season. Some of the subjects were told to gargle three times a day. At the end of the study period, the group that regularly gargled had a nearly "},{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","title":"Study abstract.","uri":null,"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240719180733\u002Fhttp:\u002F\u002Fwww.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov\u002Fpubmed\u002F16242593"}],"text":"40 percent decrease in upper respiratory tract infections compared with the control group"},{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":", and when they did get sick, “gargling tended to attenuate bronchial symptoms,” the researchers wrote."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":true,"adsDesktopHoldout":true,"adsMobile":true,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"bad":false,"index":3},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"Other studies "},{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","title":"Sstudy abstract","uri":null,"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240719180733\u002Fhttp:\u002F\u002Fwww.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov\u002Fpubmed\u002F20088240"}],"text":"have also found gargling helpful against sore throats and congestion."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"According to the Mayo Clinic, for best results, dissolve half a teaspoon of salt in a full glass of warm water and gargle the solution for a few seconds before spitting it out. Adults who want a more palatable remedy against cough and sore throat can try mixing warm water with lemon and honey. No need to spit it out."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":4},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[{"__typename":"BoldFormat","type":null}],"text":"THE BOTTOM LINE"},{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":" Gargling with a saline solution can ease symptoms of a cold."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[{"__typename":"BoldFormat","type":null}],"text":"ANAHAD O’CONNOR "},{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"scitimes@nytimes.com"}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"}]},"storyFormat":null,"storylines":[],"subsection":null,"summary":"Nothing but time can cure the common cold, but a simple cup of salt water might ease the misery this winter.","timesTags":[{"__typename":"Subject","associatedLegacyCollections":[],"displayName":"Medicine and Health","isAdvertisingBrandSensitive":false,"uri":"nyt:\u002F\u002Fsubject\u002F27b8b917-5f82-5971-a1f2-442b9d4e7374","vernacular":"disesases;health;illness;medicine"},{"__typename":"Subject","associatedLegacyCollections":[],"displayName":"Throat","isAdvertisingBrandSensitive":false,"uri":"nyt:\u002F\u002Fsubject\u002F1016771d-6692-5a10-afc1-db47c5f7163c","vernacular":"Throat"},{"__typename":"Subject","associatedLegacyCollections":[],"displayName":"Colds","isAdvertisingBrandSensitive":false,"uri":"nyt:\u002F\u002Fsubject\u002Fc16821b7-61b4-5e8d-8fe5-b3b7dc49f9d1","vernacular":"Common
